transcript.text: Hello?
Hello, Mike?
Yes, sir.
Hey there. This is Paul with Hibernia. How are you doing today?
I'm doing fantastic. How are you?
I'm doing just fine. Well, thanks for asking. How is, how's the weather in California?
You know, the crazy thing is it rained last night and I'm in <crosstalk>. Come on now. We don't see rain in July or June or May do,
Do you, do you have a few minutes? I'll tell you a funny story.
Huh? Well, let's just get through this thing and how you're gonna make my financial worries go away.

Yeah. So I mean, kind of the way we work, we have these initial calls like this, it's designed to be 15, 10 to 15 minutes just to introduce one another. Then we set up another call where in the meantime you'll send us some statements so that that way we can review it, take a look at your investments and see what you have so far. And then design a plan based on your needs and comfort level. Um, you know, if you think about investing there, everybody has a different level of risk and everybody uses different types of investments. So for some clients it could be a large cap dividend portfolio. For other clients, it could be fixed or structured notes where they pay you predetermined rates of return. Um, could be a credit situation where we're kicking out a 10% yield and clients live off of income on a monthly basis. So it, so it really depends on the type of, of, uh, situation that you're in and your comfort level of risk. And to be very candid, the dollar amount that you have to invest because, you know, if somebody has $200,000, there's frankly not a lot that we can do with 200,000. But if somebody has 2 million, then we can enact or develop a broadly diversified portfolio using various types of investment vehicles to help those clients achieve their long-term objectives.
Sure.

Um, you know, the next question that we usually get is, do you sell annuities or do you push annuities? And the answer is no. We don't push annuities. Um, we do sell annuities. If they are, if they are correct, if they're the right vehicle for that intent, that intent. But we're not out here just pushing annuities. That is not at all what we do.
Are you a fiduciary?

I am a fiduciary. Okay. We, we are an SEC registered firm. We have, we custody our assets at both Fidelity Schwab and L-P-L-L-P-L. If you're not familiar, they're a publicly traded company based in San Diego. They're gigantic. Um, you know, they're the nation's largest independent broker dealer as well. And obviously you're familiar with Schwab, you're familiar with, um, fidelity as well.
Yes.

So with that said, I mean we do, again, getting back to the investment side, it depends on your comfort level. It depends on, um, your liquidity. Uh, you know, if you are in California, probably have a pension plan, in which case you also have additional or outside assets that maybe you're not as dependent on, on drawing from. Um, and maybe, maybe the goal is to, to grow them at, you know, the next five to seven years for retirement, in which case we can then turn on an income stream. So it, uh, it depends on your situation. I see that you're currently 58 years of age. Is that correct? Yeah. Okay. And are you working or are you retired?
Oh man. Working kind of part-time.
Working part-time. Okay. Yeah. What is it that prompted you for this, for this call?
Fear. Fear.
Fear.
Fear.
Fear of the market. Fear of, yeah.
Okay. Fear. I've got, uh, I don't know, almost two, close to two in 4 0 1 Ks.
And where are those 4 0 1 Ks managers? They have fidelity?

No, no, no, no. Uh, I got, I went through the whole Fidelity thing and the thing that, just the thing that just worried me so much about going through, actually it wasn't through Fidelity, it was through Fisher. 'cause I sat down and met with them.
Yeah.

Okay. And, and the whole 1% for not doing anything. And that, that just kind of bothered me. Yeah. And, uh, the, the fear is do I have enough? How do I navigate through my sixties and that kind of stuff.
Sure. That was it.

Yeah. I'll tell you, I mean, one of the common knocks against Fed against Fisher is everybody's in the same portfolio. And they don't, the public doesn't understand that. They don't see that, but they're, they're charging a 1.5% fee and you're in an what's called an asset allocated ETF model. And, and they've been switching where you used to have individual stocks, now they're pushing everybody into ETFs. So they're charging you one point a half percent. You're in a ETF model. And you know, frankly, when the market goes down 30%, you might go down 29%. And, you know, that's not really protecting the money, so to speak. Whereas, you know, again, when I mentioned earlier the different types of investment vehicles, I'll give you a quick example. Last, last year, markets were a little volatile. Client of mine had to, had to deposit money into his, what's called a, a pension plan.

It's called a cash balance plan. And he was really worried about the market. So in his particular instance, we invested a hundred thousand dollars in a principle protected note issued by Goldman Sachs. So it's what's called PPNA principle protected note issued by Goldman Sachs. And over the next five years, he was gonna get 1.6 times the return of the s and p 500 with no downside, meaning if, if the stock market, and he happened to be 60 years old. So he was concerned about preserving his money for age 65 when he retired. Right. So I said, look, let's, we invested this money in a principle protected note issued by Goldman Sachs, which means that at retirement at age 65, he's gonna have no less than what he started with. And, and that was very comforting for him. Now, obviously, you and I both know over the last year, stock market has increased considerably, so his note is increased considerably. Right. So that's just one example of how we use different investment vehicles compared to your generic kind of cookie cutter ETF asset allocated model.
Yeah.

Um, in, in terms of your portfolio, you said right around $2 million in 4 0 1 Ks. Is that in some mutual funds that you manage or is it, is it individual stocks or can you describe a little bit about that?
Yeah, um, a million of it is, is in, um, yeah, they're all mutual funds.
Okay.
One of 'em is, uh, one of them is one of those, uh, in power, uh, date, you know, date retirement things where they say, Hey, what year you wanna retire? Uh oh. Oh,
Okay. That's called a target date fund. Right. Okay.

I knew, I knew you were gonna know what it is. I it's a date thing. So there's, uh, there's, I don't know, there's, uh, a half a million bucks in that end. And then the other part that makes up that almost million dollars is, uh, just your basic, um, large cap mutual, mutual funds. Sure. No individual stocks. And then there's a swab account that's probably got 400,000 in it. And, uh, most of those just mimic, um, what does Schwab have? Schwab has some with it, an s and p 500 mimic thing, something,
Correct. Yeah. They're e they have an ETF model that mimics the s and p 500
That Yes.
Right. And that, and that's a good

Mm-hmm <affirmative>. And then I got sucked into a, uh, uh, an annuity with Lincoln Financial and I don't know, there's $250,000 in there and then I've got another, I don't know, 80,000 sitting in the bank.
Okay. Well, um, I tell you what I mean, today was really just more of a, a quick 10, 15 minute introductory phone call,
Uhhuh <affirmative>.

I do think that we would be able to help you reviewing what you currently have, showing you a fin running a financial plan for you to give you some projections in the future, and then look at what an income plan could be when you ultimately retire. Obviously a lot's gonna change between today and when you retire, but it's a good starting point.
No, that retirement thing happens when I'm 59 and a half, so I don't have to say pay the 10% anymore.
Okay. So, so, so <laugh>. So you're

Just, that's kind of when that's happening. The only reason why I'm, I'm working part-time and everything else like that is just sitting around waiting for the 10% to go away and then all bets are off.

Well, you know, I mean, you can, um, like you can, we can turn that income stream on today and help you avoid a 10% penalty. So you don't, you don't have to sit here and wait until you're 59 and a half. I mean, we could, it's what's called a Rule 72 Q. We could turn that on, take qualified distributions. And there, there are rules. The IRS has rules around it in what you can and can't do. Yeah. So you can't just treat it like a piggy bank. But in that instance, what we typically would do is set up a five year distribution where you would take this, what's called equally and substantially the same payments. So let's just say that you took $2,000 a month every month for the next five years, that'll allow you to avoid the 10% penalty that the IRS assesses, or you don't do that and you just wait until age 59 and a half and then you can take out money without a 10% penalty. Yeah. But the rule that the IRS has is you have to take out payments for the longer of a five year period or until you turn age 59 and a half. So in your case, turning 59 and a half a year and a half from today, that's the shorter option. So you'd have to satisfy the five year hold.
Yeah, that's, that, that's, I mean, that's my plan.
Okay.
That's my plan. Somewhere around 60 I, because I don't have a house payment, I don't have any of that stuff.
Okay. So no, no house, no, no debt.
None.
Okay. Well, you're in a gr really good spot then. Do you have a, do you have a target income that you would like to solve on a monthly basis?
Um,
If today, if you were 59 and a half, how would you treat your distributions?
I, I figured it. I, I figured it out in the rough math. The rough math was somewhere between 40 and $60,000 in my pocket a year.
Okay.

So that meant that it was somewhere around 80 to a hundred thousand dollars a year that I would need, you know, figuring out the taxes and everything else because my wife's gonna, my wife continues to work 'cause she's several years younger than me.

Okay. So just to give you an example on a $2 million portfolio, I ran, I ran an illustration for a client and we set it up to where for the last 20 years we took that, we started with $1 million and we took out 7%, which would be $70,000 a year.
Yeah.

Not only did we generate over the last 20 years, 2.1 million of income, but the million dollars doubled to 2 million bucks. And so I say that because here you are today, age 58 on a $2 million portfolio, we can pretty easily generate a hundred thousand dollars a year on a pre-tax basis so that you can pay California state income tax and pay Uncle Sam and net and net that, you know, 50, $60,000 or more without having you blow up your $2 million nest egg.
That's the, uh, that's the plan.
Yeah. I mean, look, we, this is what we do on a daily basis, so I know we'd be able to help you
And, uh, the elephant in the room, what's your cut?

So we, like you mentioned earlier, you said, are we fiduciaries? The answer is yes, we are fiduciary, which means we are legally obligated to always act in your best interest, number one. Number two, we do not use any commission based products, uhhuh, which, which means we do charge a fee for, for that because that's how the SEC works. With that said, it depends on the vehicle that we're using. Meaning if it's a portfolio of dividend paying stocks, we're probably around one, one and a quarter percent. Okay. But to reduce your total fee, that's where we would include some of those notes, like those structured notes that I talked about earlier. Uhhuh
<affirmative>, the

Reason being, there's no fee, there's zero cost, there's no implicit fee associated with those. And that's our, that's our quote unquote protection that we use to where, I mean, look, the market's at an all time high right now, this is the most the stock market has ever been worth. I do think it's going to increase more, but it's gonna be really volatile in the short period of time. And so if you, if you think about the portfolio, it's not just what's the fee on the total, but it is, what's that price for that downside protection? I can tell you earlier this year in, in, um, earlier this year when the market dropped and even early last year when when we had the, uh, uh, the tariffs and the market dropped 20% uhhuh and clients were flat, they were very, very happy with that. And so that kind of, that fee question goes right out the window when the markets are down 20% in their, their level. Right.

So now mm-hmm <affirmative>. Now let's just say, to make sure I understand this correctly, if you move, let's, let's just say, let's just say you move a million dollars into, into those notes to where they're paying and that kind of stuff into which you don't charge any commission and then you manage the other $500,000 at 1%. Correct. So am I paying now, am I paying the commission on the whole, on all of it or just the part that you're actively managing?

You're paying it on the whole thing because we don't, because the notes don't have any costs associated with them. Sure. Meaning there's no brokerage fees or brokerage commissions built in. Yeah. Which mean means that you're getting the best performance.
Huh.
Okay. And you're getting the downside protection. But it does, and and I'll give you an example. Let's just say that you have two identical notes issued by Goldman Sachs Uhhuh.

One is a fee-based note and one is a commission based note, but it's the exact same terms. Okay. The commission-based note, let's just say the leverage for the s and p 500 is 1.25%, meaning you get 125% of the return of the market. Whereas in a fee-based note being a fiduciary, instead of 125%, you might get one 50 or one 60. So you're getting, even though you're paying a fee versus brokerage where you think you're not paying a fee in brokerage, you're actually paying for it out of your return because the, because Goldman Sachs gives you lower leverage, thereby lower returns on the, on the note. So, you know, and that's a, that's what's called a growth note. If I used an income note, which is designed to pay income, callable yield notes in brokerage, might, might pay six to 7%. Whereas in advisory, on the fee-based side, it's gonna pay seven to 8%. So it's the same 1% fee. Sure. Whether you, whether you see it or don't see it, it, it, Goldman Sachs just embeds that costing brokerage versus advisory. There's no cost. Sure. So that, so once I show it to clients on paper and they see it as a side by side comparison, it alleviates any concern and it makes complete sense.
Yeah. So you just make your money being the middleman. 'cause I'm sure I can't, uh, I'm sure I can't log into a website and buy one of these, uh, Goldman Sachs secured notes by myself. Can I?

No, you, you can't do that. Nor can you, I mean, look, I'm on my computer screen right now. I'm looking at a venture capital fund that invests in SpaceX and this type of stuff. And you know, it's, it's the same, same situation, right? I mean, we do manage money in the, in the sense that we pick individual stocks and use, uh, use our, our models, but obviously we don't invest directly in SpaceX and stuff of that nature. So we are what you would say, a middleman for those kind of alternative investments like venture capital, private equity, or structured notes and so forth. Yeah. So, you know, so yes. Um, and, and, and again, on our next call when we get together, we'll be able to show you these different types of investments because they all have different risk profiles associated with them. It's, and what we're looking for or trying to achieve is that asymmetric risk where you're taking less risk and you're getting better returns. So your risk adjusted returns are much better. Right. Because, and,
And there's nothing proprietary about your, uh, your software, your algorithm.

I mean, we, so we don't use an algorithm. Sure. We perform our own research, uhhuh <affirmative>, and, and now I will say this, we do, in your case where it's all retirement money, this doesn't pertain to you, but if you had, say for example, you had $800,000 in the bank versus 80,000 and we wanted to go and invest $50,000 in a, in a stock portfolio, but we use a, a long short strategy over at Schwab where we're a hundred percent long, the portfolio on the upside, and we can short up to 30% of the portfolio on the downside. So in your instance where your concern is preservation, we can have what's, we can dial the risk down or up and have what's called a market neutral stance where, hey, things look a little, a little volatile, so let's, let's get neutral, but then when things look good, let's dial up the risk and have a more growth tilt to the portfolio. So kind of think of it as, think of your car having cruise control. If you're going up the mountain, you have to give it more gas. If you're going down the mountain, you let off the gas and you kind of coast. Right? Yeah. So it's kind of the same way of, of thinking about investing.
Yeah.

And, and believe me, that is a very different strategy than buy the s and p 500 and, and get on the roller coaster. Right. That, that doesn't do, that doesn't do any good. And frankly, when you're on that rollercoaster, well,
It's been, it's been great for the last 10, 15 years for me.
Absolutely. Look, the stock market's performed very

Well and I, and I just look at, and I just look at over that, over that last 10 or 15 years, I would've given away, or I would've paid away 20 KA year or 10 KA year. So over 10 years, that's a hundred thousand dollars.

Look, I hear you. Um, however, I'll, I'll say this. When you look at these growth notes that we, that we did, I mean, just yesterday, now this is actually in a, a link in annuity that I have, but just yesterday I did a, a, um, a, a report for a client of mine and I sent it to him over the last year, the s and p 500 was up 20% and he was up 23.8%. So in that instance, we're beating the market by almost 4% over the last year. And that does warrant a 1% fee. Yeah,
Yeah. Absolutely.

And, and I'll tell you, with these growth notes like Goldman Sachs and stuff, we, we do, you know, we do, I don't wanna say always beat the market because there is the opportunity to potentially lose money, but we have outperformed the market just because of the leverage that we have in those notes. It's really that, that simple. And so, you're right, we do act as a middleman in the structured note capacity. Yeah.
But

You know, frankly, you're getting better performance than, than you can. And not to mention in your case where you're concerned about loss, those notes have typically a 30% barrier to the downside, which means I, I don't have a crystal ball and I don't know where the market's gonna be in five years, but in five years, if the market is down 20%, then this note is gonna return a hundred percent of your money because a 20% decline falls within that 30% barrier.
Sure.
So it's just, you know, again, I mean this is what we do is not your asset allocated ETF model. It's, it's, it's more complex, more moving parts. And frankly, it's, you know, that's what you pay for.
Is that, yeah.
Um, would I, do you have your calendar in front of you so we could schedule another call?
Um, it would be any, it would be any Tuesday or Thursday.
Any Tuesday or Thursday.
Yeah.
Okay. What about, what about this Thursday?
Uh, this Thursday? This Thursday's? No, I, I,
Okay.
Got a fishing trip booked, but next Tuesday is fine.
Oh, um, I tell you the truth, actually next Tuesday I'm gonna be in New York. What about Tuesday the 19th?
That's fine.
All right, so let's do this Tuesday the 19th. Is this a good time for you?
Perfect time.

8:00 AM Yeah. All right. So well, I'll schedule it for Tuesday the 19th at 8:00 AM Can I send you a Zoom link so that, that way you can log in on the computer and I'll be able to show you my computer screen.
Okay. And you'll be
Able to see exactly what I'm talking about with these notes and stuff of that nature.
Uhhuh <affirmative>.

Okay. All right then. Let's do that. I'll, I'll, um, I'll have my admin send out a Zoom link. I'll send out a, a little bio a flyer for us, just so you can see the firm, who we are. Um, we do, we do have 74 clients in California. Um, all through, all the way down from San Diego, Temecula, Oceanside, all the way up to Chico, Tahoe, big Bear, you know, all, all around, uh, I mean, honestly, I had in the, I had an office in, in San Diego until COVID, and then we shut it down because everything was virtual. So we do have a pretty large presence on the west Coast. But in the meantime, I'll send you a Zoom link for Tuesday, the 19th at 10, at, uh, 10:00 AM e uh, Pacific.
No, 8:00 AM Pacific.
I'm sorry, 8:00 AM Pacific, 10:00 AM Central. That's correct. Yeah.
Okay.
Okay. Sounds good. Well
Appreciate,
Sounds good. Thank you.

review.summary: The advisor demonstrated strong product knowledge and successfully scheduled a follow-up meeting, but the call was heavily focused on feature-selling structured notes and fee explanations rather than uncovering the prospect’s deeper emotional and financial pain points. While the prospect expressed fear about market volatility and retirement readiness, the advisor accepted these surface-level answers and immediately pivoted to product pitches, missing the opportunity to explore what success or failure looks like for the client. To improve, the advisor should ask open-ended questions about the prospect’s ideal retirement lifestyle and specific goals before offering solutions, and avoid discussing fees until value is clearly established.

categories.name: The advisor should find deep pain points for the prospect to
categories.feedback: The advisor identified level 1 pain (fear of the market, fear of not having enough) but did not dig deeper to uncover level 2 or level 3 pain. They accepted the surface-level answers and moved on to discussing products and fees, missing the opportunity to explore the emotional or deeper financial concerns behind the prospect's fear.

categories.name: The advisor should confidently inform the prospect of next s
categories.feedback: The advisor did a good job explaining the next steps and tying them to the prospect's concerns about fear and retirement income, but the explanation of fees and benefits was somewhat lengthy and could have been more concise. The advisor successfully scheduled the next call with a specific date and time, and offered to send a Zoom link, which is positive. However, the transition to scheduling could have been smoother, as it came after a long discussion about products rather than a clear summary of how the next call would directly address the prospect's needs.

categories.name: The advisor should avoid discussing fees on the first call b
categories.feedback: The advisor discussed fees extensively on the first call, including specific percentages and fee structures, which violates the criteria of avoiding fee discussions until value is presented. The advisor should have deflected the fee question and transitioned back to understanding the prospect's needs and outcomes.

categories.name: The advisor should never feature sell, which is defined as t
categories.feedback: The advisor spent the majority of the call feature selling—discussing structured notes, principal protected notes, and various investment vehicles—without first deeply exploring the prospect's desired outcomes. The prospect mentioned 'fear' and wanting to navigate retirement, but the advisor did not ask what success or failure looks like for them; instead, they immediately jumped into product pitches. To improve, the advisor should ask open-ended questions about the prospect's ideal retirement lifestyle and specific goals before offering any solutions.

criteria.description: The advisor should find deep pain points for the prospect to solve. I use a framework of level 1, level 2, and level 3 pain. - Level 1 pain, for example, could be "I want to save money on taxes." - Level 2 would be "I want to save money on taxes because it's digging into my income and I don't want to have to go back to work because I already retired." - Level 3 pain would be "I want to save money on taxes because it's digging into my income and I don't want to have to go back to work because my grandparents worked for my entire childhood and I watched them literally work until the day they died. I never got to spend any time with them and that's not what I want for my grandkids." The advisor should never accept a level one answer without digging into it immediately. If they don't dig into it immediately, they definitely need to circle back and touch on that later in the conversation. They should get a minimum of level two pain. If they get level three pain, this is a nine or ten out of ten
criteria.success_condition: The advisor identified pain points at level one and understood that that was an indicator to dig deeper. They dug deeper into those points and got level two or level three pain. - If they accepted level one answers, this is a three or lower. - If they asked more questions but didn't get satisfying answers, this is a five or lower. - If they identify level one pain, ask questions, and got level two, this is an eight or lower. - Or if they got all the way to level three, this is a nine or a ten.

criteria.description: The advisor should confidently inform the prospect of next steps in the sales process and frame it in such a way that taking those next steps is tied to the prospect solving problems or getting what they want. The advisor should make the transition to the next step in the sales process, booking the next call, as seamless as possible. The advisor should always get a call on the calendar with an invitation sent to the prospect. The advisor should never get off the phone without proposing a next step at the very least and if the prospect agrees to the next step, the advisor should never accept ending the conversation without scheduling that next step
criteria.success_condition: The advisor confidently explained the next steps, explained the benefits that the prospect would get from those next steps, or framed it in such a way that the prospect understands this is to help them, and then scheduled the next meeting with a time, date, and invitation sent

criteria.description: The advisor should avoid discussing fees on the first call because they have not had the opportunity to present any value yet. Talking about pricing before receiving value consistently leads to objections. If asked about fees, the advisor should deflect the question. If pushed on that deflection, the advisor should give a broad range with the caveat that fees are dependent on what solutions make the most sense. Before they can prescribe any solutions, they must first understand problems and transition back into having a conversation about the outcomes that the prospect wants
criteria.success_condition: The advisor does not discuss fees

criteria.description: The advisor should never feature sell, which is defined as talking a lot about products or services that they can offer or how their firm is different without understanding what the prospect is looking for. This is an approach of throwing things at the wall and seeing what sticks and does not effectively close deals. The advisor should always ask the prospect what they are looking for or what outcomes they want and ask them to clearly define what those outcomes look like. What does success look like? What does failure look like? Speak to these specific outcomes that the prospect is looking for rather than blindly talking about features
criteria.success_condition: The advisor only talks about features or products that are directly relevant to outcomes that the prospect has defined. To be very clear here this is not about problems that they have; it is about outcomes that they want. Instead of a prospect saying that they want to save money on taxes and the advisor recommending some tax-efficient investment vehicles, what should happen instead is the advisor should ask questions about: - why they want to save money on taxes - what they would like to do with that money instead - what an ideal outcome looks like - what a failure mode outcome looks like and then make recommendations based off of exactly what the prospect described to them
